dried-leaf.label: Dried Leaf
summary: Dried and crispy leaves are usually a sign of water stress, not a disease.
Sunburn from full midday sun on young trees will dry leaf edges and tips fast.
A salty drink of water from over-fertilization or hard irrigation water can look identical to drought.
Root rot in waterlogged soil produces dried leaves even when the ground is soaking wet.
Wind steals moisture from leaves faster than the roots can refill them, especially in the dry season.
Bark borers and scale insects can cut off a branch's water supply, drying leaves from the tip back.
Magnesium and other micronutrient deficiencies make leaves more brittle and prone to crisping.
Shallow watering is a common cause, because it trains the roots to stay near the surface where they dry first.
Heat radiating off concrete, metal roofs, or gravel can scorch a nearby tree before you notice.
Potted lanzones dry out much faster than trees in the ground, especially in dark pots.
Transplant shock often shows up as a sudden wave of dried leaves in the first few weeks.
Drought stress compounds over time, so a few missed watering days in a heat wave can show up later as sudden leaf drop.
Bringing back consistent soil moisture is the main fix, not soaking the foliage.
Deep, slow watering beats frequent shallow watering every time.
A thick mulch layer is one of the single biggest wins for keeping leaves from drying out.
Partial shade for young trees prevents most sun-related leaf drying.
Severely stressed trees should not be fertilized until they rehydrate.
Thinning dead branches and heavy fruit loads lets the tree focus on saving the leaves it still has.
Pattern matters. Tip drying points to drought, edge drying points to salt, between-vein drying points to nutrients.
Recovery is possible for most trees if you act early. Once more than half the canopy is crispy, expect partial dieback.
tips: Before you reach for the hose, poke a finger 5 to 10 cm into the soil. If it still feels damp, hold off on watering.
Water slowly and deeply so the moisture actually reaches the roots. Quick shallow watering just trains roots to stay near the surface where they dry out fastest.
In the dry season, water young trees every 2 or 3 days, and give mature trees a good soak once a week.
Lay down a thick 3 to 4 inch layer of mulch around the base. Rice hull, dried grass, or coconut coir all work well for holding moisture in.
Pull the mulch back about 5 to 10 cm from the trunk itself. Mulch piled against the bark invites collar rot.
Young lanzones really prefer 30 to 50 percent shade for the first few years. Midday sun will scorch those tender leaves.
Shade cloth works, but so does planting under bananas or coconut, or tucking the tree next to something taller.
Avoid windy spots if you can. Wind pulls water out of leaves faster than the roots can keep up.
If the leaves are drying even though the soil is wet, check the roots. Waterlogged roots can't absorb water, so it looks just like drought.
In heavy clay, plant on a raised mound and work in plenty of organic matter so water can actually drain away.
Prune the dead crispy branches back to healthy wood. No point asking the tree to keep supplying water to wood that's already gone.
Feed with a balanced 14-14-14 or 15-15-15 fertilizer every 3 months. A well-fed tree bounces back from dry spells much faster.
Throw in some magnesium and trace nutrients too. Magnesium deficiency shows up as yellowing between the veins that eventually turns brown and crispy.
Don't fertilize a tree that's already badly stressed. Wait until it's rehydrated, otherwise the salt will just burn the leaves more.
Watch out for reflected heat near concrete walls, driveways, or metal roofs. Those surfaces cook young trees.
For potted lanzones, never let the pot go bone dry. Drip trays or self-watering saucers help a lot during hot months.
Every few weeks, flush potted trees with plenty of water to wash out salts that build up and burn the leaf tips.
Check the trunk and branches for bark borer or scale damage. Girdled branches can't move water, so they dry from the tip backward.
A lime wash on the lower trunk reflects sun and protects the cambium from scald, which is a sneaky cause of dried leaves higher up.
Keep a simple watering journal for the first year. Logging rainfall and your own watering helps you dial things in before the tree starts complaining.
Drip irrigation or even just 2 or 3 emitters per tree takes the guesswork out of consistent moisture.
Bury a short piece of PVC pipe near the roots so you can pour water straight down to depth during heat waves.
Don't let lawn grass grow right up to the trunk. Turf roots are aggressive and will outcompete a young tree for water.
If tips are crispy but leaf bases are green, suspect salt burn from too much fertilizer or hard water. Flush the basin with 2 or 3 volumes of clean water.
Test your irrigation water once a year. Lanzones handles EC up to about 1.5 dS/m, and things go downhill past that.
During heat waves, a quick canopy mist early in the morning briefly raises humidity and cools the leaves.
Shade newly transplanted trees for at least two weeks with palm fronds, banana leaves, or netting.
Try to transplant at the start of the rainy season so the tree gets months of natural watering to settle in.
Water the roots, not the trunk base. Keeping the trunk dry avoids collar rot while the leaves still get what they need.
When trees share a bed, water the whole bed as one unit so no single tree dries out faster than the others.
Leaf damage patterns tell a story. Drying from the tip usually means drought, edges inward usually means salt, and between the veins usually means nutrients.
If a stressed tree is holding a heavy fruit load, thin some of it out. It can't keep both the foliage and the fruit alive at once.
A gentle foliar feed of seaweed or kelp every 10 days helps a recovering tree without piling on more salt.
Check the trunk for tiny pinholes from ambrosia beetles if leaves dry in patches. Vascular pests can look just like drought.
Mixing biochar into sandy soil helps the root zone hang onto water much longer.
In clay, plant on a 30 cm raised mound so the crown stays drained even in heavy rain.
Group lanzones with humidity lovers like banana, coffee, or cacao. Together they build a moister microclimate.
Cheap soil moisture probes or tensiometers beat guessing. Let the numbers tell you when to water.
Cover bare soil with a living ground cover like peanut vine or sweet potato. It shades and cools the root zone.
In hot weather, prune in the late afternoon. Fresh cuts in midday sun dry out fast.
Seal bigger pruning wounds with tree paint or diluted copper paste to keep pathogens and drying out.
Don't mess with the soil or prune heavy roots within 2 meters of the trunk. Damaged roots can't keep leaves hydrated.
Water the day before and the day after you fertilize. The nutrients dissolve properly and don't pull moisture out of leaves.
If the tree is potted, repot every 2 or 3 years. Old media loses its structure and dries in minutes.
Pick light-colored or glazed pots. Dark pots can hit 50 °C in the sun and literally cook the feeder roots.
Build a windbreak on the windward side. Hot dry wind can dry leaves faster than you can water them.
Change only one thing at a time. If you water more, fertilize, and foliar feed all at once, you won't know what helped.
Check leaves early in the morning. If they already look limp before the sun hits, the tree ran short overnight and needs a deep water today.
When in doubt, water the soil, not the leaves. Hydrated roots will fix dried foliage from the inside out.
For a severely wilted tree, rehydrate in stages. One deep soak, wait a day, then another soak. Sudden flooding shocks stressed roots.

healthy.label: Healthy
summary: A healthy lanzones tree has deep green leaves, a clean trunk, and no sticky honeydew or white dust on the foliage.
New flushes come in regularly, and leaves sit flat rather than curling or drooping.
Consistent care matters more than intensive care. The tree rewards steady habits.
Soil between pH 6.0 and 6.5 with good drainage is the baseline for long-term health.
Balanced NPK every few months plus micronutrients once a year covers most nutritional needs.
Organic matter in the soil, whether from compost, manure, or vermicast, keeps the root zone alive.
Deep watering once or twice a week encourages strong downward root growth.
Mulch is cheap insurance. It moderates soil temperature and moisture at the same time.
Partial shade for the first few years protects young trees from sunburn and heat stress.
Lanzones fruits on old wood, so light pruning is better than aggressive pruning.
Good airflow through the canopy prevents most fungal issues before they start.
A weekly walkthrough of the orchard catches pest and disease pressure early.
Beneficial insects like lady beetles and parasitic wasps do a lot of free pest control if you don't spray them out.
Windbreaks protect flowers and young fruit from the biggest threat to yield, which is flower drop.
Pollinator access during flowering directly drives fruit set.
Cut fruit clusters by the stalk at harvest so the bearing wood stays intact for next year.
Post-harvest rest with a gentle foliar feed sets up the next cycle.
Record keeping, even just dates and yields per tree, is what turns gardening into real orchard management.
A healthy tree is more forgiving of occasional weather stress, missed watering, or minor pest pressure.
Over-intervention, not neglect, is the most common cause of sudden decline in otherwise healthy trees.
tips: Aim for slightly acidic, well-drained soil between pH 6.0 and 6.5. Test once a year and adjust with sulfur or lime as needed.
Water deeply once or twice a week instead of shallow daily sprinkles. Deep watering sends roots downward where they belong.
Feed a balanced 14-14-14 or 15-15-15 fertilizer every 3 months. Spread it around the drip line rather than dumping it at the trunk.
Bump up potassium before and during fruit development. A little 0-0-60 or sulfate of potash makes a real difference in sugar and yield.
Add micronutrients yearly. Magnesium, boron, and zinc are silent yield limiters even when the leaves look green.
Work in compost, aged manure, or vermicast every year. It keeps the soil alive and well structured.
Keep a permanent 3 to 4 inch mulch ring under the canopy. Top it up as it breaks down.
Hand-weed within a meter of the trunk. Bolos and string trimmers chew up the shallow roots in a heartbeat.
Prune lightly. Lanzones fruits on old wood, so you just want to take out dead, crossing, or sick branches.
Thin out crowded inner branches to let air move through. That one habit prevents most fungal problems.
Wipe pruning tools with alcohol or diluted bleach between trees. You don't want to spread anything you're cutting out.
For the first 3 to 5 years, give the tree partial shade, around 30 to 50 percent. Full sun too early just slows things down.
When you stake a young tree, keep the ties loose. Tight stakes girdle the trunk as it thickens.
Do a weekly walkthrough. Scan for scale, borer frass, or leaf spots. Catching things early beats fixing them late, every single time.
Let the ladybugs and parasitic wasps stay. Skip the broad-spectrum sprays on healthy trees and they'll handle a lot of pest pressure for you.
Put up a windbreak to protect flowers and young fruit. Flower drop is the biggest reason a good tree gives a disappointing harvest.
Bag developing fruit clusters with paper or netting close to harvest. It saves you from fruit flies and bats.
Harvest whole clusters by cutting the stalk, not by plucking single fruits. Pulling damages the bearing wood you'll need next year.
After harvest, give the tree a break with a gentle foliar of seaweed or compost tea. It helps kickstart next season.
Keep records. Fertilizer dates, bloom times, yield per tree. The pattern is what shows you what a healthy tree looks like on your specific site.
Line up big fertilizer applications with the start of the rainy season so the nutrients move into the soil naturally.
Split the annual fertilizer into 3 or 4 smaller doses. One giant dose leaches and shocks the tree.
Broadcast fertilizer in a shallow trench along the drip line, not at the trunk. That's where the feeder roots actually are.
Top-dress 5 to 10 kg of well-rotted manure per mature tree at the start of the wet season. It's a slow, forgiving source of nitrogen.
Work in 2 or 3 kg of wood ash per mature tree once a year. It adds potassium and nudges acidic soils upward a bit.
A little Epsom salt each year, around 100 g per tree, keeps leaves dark green and helps fruit sugars load.
Monthly compost tea or EM drench during the growing season builds up the life in the root zone.
Nitrogen-fixing ground covers like Arachis pintoi or Desmodium between trees quietly build fertility for you.
Protect the trunk from weed trimmer damage. A simple plastic guard or a ring of stones saves trees.
Thin crowded flower clusters just a little. Fewer but larger fruits often pay better, and you leave 60 to 70 percent of the panicles.
Clean off sucker shoots and low branches below about 60 cm each year. It shapes up a proper single-trunk tree.
Paint the trunk with a simple 1:10 lime and water mix each dry season. It deters borers and reflects heat.
If fruit set is low, try hand-pollinating with a soft brush. Some areas just have shy flowers.
Keep beehives or native stingless bees within 200 m of the orchard if you can. Fruit set improves noticeably.
Bag ripening clusters in paper or cloth 3 to 4 weeks before harvest to cut losses from fruit flies and rats.
Don't spray broad-spectrum insecticides during flowering. Losing pollinators hurts more than most pests would have.
Yellow sticky traps and pheromone lures let you spot scale and fruit fly pressure before it becomes a real outbreak.
If scale does flare up, the predator beetle Chilocorus nigrita has a solid track record on Philippine lanzones.
Mow cover crops between trees a couple of times a year. Bare-soiling the orchard creates more problems than it solves.
Harvest fruit in the cool of the morning and shade it right away. Shelf life starts the moment you pick.
Test soil every 2 or 3 years for pH, organic matter, and key nutrients. Fertilize based on the results, not on habit.
Aerate any compacted spots with a pitchfork or auger about 30 cm from the trunk every couple of years.
A thin yearly layer of carbonized rice hull improves soil tilth and slowly feeds silica.
Keep heavy equipment off the root zone. Compaction is a quiet long-term killer that's hard to reverse.
Draw a simple orchard map and tag each tree. Casual observation turns into real data pretty fast.
Photograph each tree monthly from the same spot. Year-over-year changes jump out when you compare photos.
Rotate through trees on your weekly walk. Five minutes of close attention per tree catches most problems early.
Keep a tree first-aid kit ready. Pruning shears, copper fungicide, horticultural oil, wound paint, and mulch. That covers most scouting finds.
Partner lanzones with banana or gliricidia instead of relying on shade nets forever. Living shade beats plastic.
Celebrate a healthy tree by mostly leaving it alone. Over-intervention causes more sudden declines than neglect ever does.

leaf-rust.label: Leaf Rust
summary: Rust is a fungal disease caused by airborne spores that settle on wet leaves.
The classic sign is orange, yellow, or brown pustules, usually first on the underside of the leaf.
Warm humid weather is when rust spreads fastest, especially after long rains.
Stagnant air inside a dense canopy is what lets spores germinate and spread.
Wet leaves at dusk are the ideal landing strip for rust spores.
Rust overwinters on fallen infected leaves, so sanitation matters.
Spores travel on wind, tools, clothing, and splashing rain.
Soft new growth from too much nitrogen is much more vulnerable than mature leaves.
Severe infections defoliate branches, weaken the tree, and reduce yield the following season.
Early intervention matters. The first orange dot is the signal to start treatment.
Sanitation, airflow, and well-timed fungicide sprays are the three legs of rust management.
Prune the inner canopy to let air and light through.
Water at the base, not overhead, and water in the morning if you must wet the leaves.
Rotate at least 2 or 3 fungicide modes of action across a season to avoid resistance.
Copper-based fungicides are good protectants, while systemics like myclobutanil and propiconazole are curative.
Organic programs can lean on sulfur, Bacillus subtilis, and potassium silicate.
Destroy infected leaves rather than compost them.
Strip heavily infected branches instead of trying to save them.
Some lanzones cultivars handle rust better than others, so genetics matter in rust-prone areas.
Rust tends to recur in the same areas each year, so build prevention into the wet-season calendar.
tips: The moment you see orange, yellow, or brown pustules, pull those leaves off and get rid of them. Don't compost them. Burn or bury them instead.
Open up the inner canopy with a light prune. Rust spores love still, humid pockets of air.
Plant trees 6 to 8 meters apart so the canopies don't grow into each other and trap moisture.
Water at the base of the tree. Wet leaves at dusk are basically a landing strip for rust spores.
If you have to water from above, do it first thing in the morning so leaves dry fast.
Rake up infected fallen leaves every week during an outbreak. Rust overwinters on debris.
When spores first show up, start a preventive fungicide program. Myclobutanil, propiconazole, or azoxystrobin all work well.
Rotate fungicide modes of action every 2 or 3 sprays. Resistance builds up fast if you lean on one product.
For organic programs, sulfur or Bacillus subtilis on a 7 to 14 day cycle works reasonably well during humid weather.
Coverage matters. Wet the undersides of leaves. That's where pustules actually form.
Don't spray sulfur in the heat of the day above about 32 °C. It will burn the leaves.
Alcohol-wipe your pruning tools between cuts when you're removing infected wood.
Go easy on nitrogen during rust pressure. Soft new growth is much more vulnerable than mature leaves.
Silicon and potassium stiffen up leaf cuticles and slow spore penetration, so keep those in the feeding program.
Drain the root zone well. Chronically wet roots produce weak foliage that rust walks right through.
Ask your local agriculture office about rust-tolerant lanzones cultivars. In rust-prone areas, genetics matter.
Mow nearby grasses and weeds that can host rust species. Breaking the spore cycle helps more than you'd think.
Watch the forecast. Hit the trees with a protective spray ahead of long humid stretches, not after.
Quarantine new nursery stock for 2 or 3 weeks before planting it near mature trees. You don't want to import spores.
Log outbreaks, sprays, and the weather. Patterns show up fast and tell you exactly when to spray next year.
Check both sides of leaves every week in wet season. The first sign of rust is tiny yellow dots on the underside.
Strip heavily infected branches completely. Trying to save them just releases more spores onto healthy leaves.
Don't pile rust-infected prunings near the orchard. Cart them out or burn them on the spot.
Try not to work in the orchard when leaves are wet. You'll carry spores tree to tree on your clothes and tools.
Wash boots and clothes after working in an infected block before you walk into clean ones.
Wipe rake tines, pruners, and ladders with alcohol between trees.
When risk is high, like warm humid rainy nights, shorten your fungicide interval from 14 days to 7 or 10.
Add a sticker-spreader to every fungicide spray. Waxy lanzones leaves shed water otherwise.
Calibrate your sprayer before every application. Under-dosing creates resistance faster than over-dosing.
Early timing beats heavy volume. The first orange dot is your signal, not a reason to wait and see.
Copper-based fungicides like copper hydroxide or copper oxychloride are solid early-season protectants.
Rotate copper with sulfur to cover both protective and curative modes.
A monthly foliar of potassium silicate toughens leaf cuticles against rust penetration.
Keep nitrogen moderate in wet season. Lush flushes of new growth are exactly what rust wants.
Lift the canopy skirt at least 1 meter off the ground. Rain splash won't carry soil spores up to the leaves as easily.
Mulch thickly to break up rain splash. If the ground stays covered, spores don't ricochet onto low leaves.
After a long rain, check 10 or 20 random leaves per tree. That tells you fast whether you need to spray.
Clear weedy grasses around the orchard. Some grasses host Puccinia species that also hit fruit trees.
In nurseries, use fans and vent windows through warm humid nights. That's when spore release peaks.
Space nursery seedlings widely on the benches so air moves between the trays.
Avoid dense intercrops on the windward side. They block airflow into the lanzones canopy.
Orient rows with the prevailing wind so leaves dry faster after rain.
Inspect 7 days after each spray. New pustules still showing up means it's time to switch active ingredients.
Don't spray fungicide in the rain or within about 6 hours of expected rain. It'll just wash off.
Wear gloves, goggles, and a mask when you spray, especially with sulfur and systemic products.
Respect the pre-harvest interval on the label. For most products it's at least 14 days.
Organic farms can get decent rust suppression from rotating Bacillus subtilis and potassium bicarbonate through mild seasons.
Build up the soil microbes with compost and mycorrhizal inoculants. A strong rhizosphere correlates with less foliar disease.
After a heavy outbreak, do a voluntary light defoliation late in the season with a trim prune. Next spring's flush starts cleaner.
In the morning, scout uphill and upwind first. Spores drift downhill and downwind through the day, so starting there catches new pustules on fresh tissue.

powdery-mildew.label: Powdery Mildew
summary: Powdery mildew shows up as a white, flour-like coating on leaves, shoots, and sometimes flowers.
It prefers dry days with humid nights, which is the opposite of most fungal diseases.
The youngest, softest growth is colonized first and shows symptoms within days.
Unlike rust, mildew doesn't need leaves to be wet. Overnight humidity alone is enough.
Stagnant air is the single biggest environmental cause of outbreaks.
The powder you see is spores, and shaking a branch releases clouds of them.
Severe infections cause distorted growth, early leaf drop, and reduced fruit quality.
Shade, crowding, and poor airflow make everything worse.
Over-fertilization with nitrogen speeds up susceptibility by pushing soft new growth.
Drought-stressed trees are more vulnerable, contrary to the myth that dry soil prevents mildew.
Early removal of infected tissue is the fastest way to slow spread.
Potassium bicarbonate, sulfur, and oils all work well for active infections when applied carefully.
Oils and sulfur must never be sprayed within 14 days of each other.
Systemic fungicides like myclobutanil, propiconazole, or tebuconazole handle heavier outbreaks.
Baking soda and milk sprays are cheap home remedies that suppress light infections.
Biologicals like Bacillus subtilis and Bacillus amyloliquefaciens make solid preventive layers.
Good airflow, whether from pruning, spacing, or even a fan in a greenhouse, disrupts the fungus's favorite conditions.
Weekly scouting during risky weather catches outbreaks early, while they are still manageable.
Some varieties resist mildew much better than others, so replacement is worth considering for chronic cases.
Mildew almost always comes back, so build prevention into your annual rhythm rather than treating it as a one-time problem.
tips: As soon as the white powder shows, cut off the affected leaves, shoots, and flowers. The fungus starts sporulating within days.
Don't compost any of it. Bag it up or burn it to keep it from coming back.
Open the canopy with a light prune. Stagnant humid night air is the number one mildew trigger.
Give trees enough room that canopies don't touch. Good airflow dries the overnight dew fast.
Skip late afternoon overhead watering. The evening humidity is what feeds the mildew, not the water.
Plant where the tree gets at least 4 to 6 hours of direct sun. Shady damp corners are mildew paradise.
Dial back nitrogen fertilizer during outbreaks. Soft new growth is the most susceptible tissue.
A homemade baking soda spray works well. Mix 1 tablespoon baking soda and half a teaspoon of liquid soap in a gallon of water, then spray both sides of the leaves weekly.
Potassium bicarbonate, like MilStop, is a stronger knockdown for active mildew than plain baking soda.
Neem oil, jojoba oil, or horticultural oil every 7 to 14 days covers both prevention and cure. The oil smothers existing colonies.
Never spray oils when it's over 30 to 32 °C or on a drought-stressed tree. Leaf burn is almost guaranteed.
Sulfur-based fungicides handle powdery mildew well, especially if you catch it early.
For heavier infections, rotate systemic fungicides like myclobutanil, propiconazole, or tebuconazole at label rates.
A diluted milk spray, about 1 part milk to 9 parts water, weekly can suppress mildew on ornamentals and some fruit trees.
Biological fungicides like Bacillus subtilis or Bacillus amyloliquefaciens give you a gentle preventive layer when used on a schedule.
Clear weeds and alternate hosts around the drip line. A lot of grasses and ornamentals carry the same mildew species.
When replanting, look for resistant varieties. Some lanzones cultivars and related Lansium types hold up better than others.
Wipe pruning tools with alcohol between plants so you're not spreading spores mechanically.
Keep the tree well watered. Drought-stressed trees are more vulnerable to mildew, despite the myth that dry soil prevents it.
Watch nightly humidity. If humidity above 70 percent lines up with daytime temps of 15 to 27 °C, pre-treat before you see symptoms.
Start stripping from the shoot tips. Mildew spreads toward new growth, not backward into old wood.
Bag removed infected material in sealed plastic before walking it out. Loose piles in a wheelbarrow drop spores everywhere.
Don't shake mildewed branches. The spores release as a cloud of fine powder and can travel meters.
Prune infected tips first thing in the morning while dew is still holding the spores down.
Keep a separate pair of pruners for mildew work and dip them in 70 percent alcohol between cuts.
If one branch is infected, cut 15 cm below the visible mildew. The hyphae extend past what you can see.
Don't crowd pots or young trees on a patio or balcony. Stagnant air is the single biggest cause of mildew in those spaces.
A small fan aimed at indoor or greenhouse plants keeps the air moving. Mildew can't really colonize moving air.
Where mildew has a history, switch to drip or soaker hoses and ditch the overhead sprinklers.
If you must water from above, mid-morning is your window. Leaves dry well before the humid evening.
On warm dry days followed by cool humid nights, nudge irrigation up a little. Healthy turgid leaves resist infection better.
Rotate at least 3 fungicide modes of action across a season so you don't breed local resistance.
Systemic fungicides like propiconazole and myclobutanil move through the leaf, so they're more forgiving if spray coverage isn't perfect.
Contact fungicides like sulfur and copper only work where they land. Take your time and hit the undersides, tips, and axils.
Oil sprays don't mix with sulfur. Leave at least 14 days between an oil application and a sulfur one.
Spray in the cool of early morning or late afternoon. Mildew is most active in low heat and high humidity, so that's when you want to hit it.
A surfactant or a quarter-percent liquid soap helps sprays spread across the waxy lanzones leaf.
Organic growers get good results from weekly potassium bicarbonate plus monthly Bacillus-based biofungicides. Layered defense beats any single product.
Don't mix multiple sprays in one tank without a jar compatibility test. Some combinations clog nozzles or burn leaves.
Fertilize lower nitrogen and higher potassium during the mildew-prone months to slow down soft growth.
Test-spray any new product on one branch and wait 72 hours before treating the whole tree.
Whey or skim milk spray is a cheap organic preventive where baking soda is hard to find.
Keep grass and weeds short around the base of the tree. Tall weeds hold overnight humidity right where you don't want it.
If you can, don't plant lanzones next to chronic mildew hosts like mango, grape, squash, or rose.
For greenhouse lanzones, aim for 50 to 70 percent relative humidity and at least one air change per minute.
Sweep up fallen leaves every week during outbreaks. The fungus can overwinter on dry debris.
Check fresh flushes most carefully. The youngest leaves are colonized first and show symptoms within days.
Don't hit a mildew-stressed tree with fresh nitrogen until the outbreak is under control.
Expect recurrence. Once a farm has mildew, it tends to come back every season. Build prevention into the annual calendar instead of reacting to each outbreak.
If a tree keeps catching mildew year after year despite good care, consider replacing it with a less susceptible variety. Some battles aren't worth fighting forever.
